title: Two rainy seasons: April to May and August to November, with October the worst
tidbit: Barranquilla has a bimodal rain pattern. The first short rainy spell runs April through May; the main season runs August through November with October averaging around 280 mm of rainfall over 15 rainy days. Flooding is the practical risk, not gentle showers. Plan around the calendar: the December to March dry season overlaps with Carnival (February) and is the easiest time to be in the city.

title: The UV index hits 11 to 14 in Barranquilla: plan outdoor activity before 10 a.m.
tidbit: Barranquilla sits near sea level at about 10 degrees north latitude. From January through May the UV index regularly reaches 11 to 14, classified as extreme. Unprotected fair skin can burn in under 15 minutes at noon. Locals and experienced expats schedule outdoor exercise, market trips, and walking before 10 a.m. or after 4 p.m. Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50 and a hat; do not skip either.

title: December is Barranquilla's most social month: novenas, street parties, and cumbia until Christmas
tidbit: December in Barranquilla is the start of the dry season and the most festive urban month outside Carnival. The novenas de aguinaldos run December 16 to 24: each night, neighborhoods and apartment complexes gather to sing, eat buñuelos and natilla, and party. Dia de las Velitas on December 7 fills windows with candles and farolitos (lanterns). Outdoor concerts and street parties run until late every weekend. The city is at its most open and social during this period, and the weather is dry enough for genuine outdoor life.

title: Hurricane season June to November affects the Colombian Caribbean coast indirectly: heavy rain and swells
tidbit: Colombia's Caribbean coast sits at the southern edge of the Atlantic hurricane belt. Direct landfalls on Barranquilla are extremely rare, but hurricane activity in the Caribbean (June to November, peak August to October) drives the heavy rainfall, flooding rains, and rough coastal swells that make this the worst season for both city life and beach day trips. When a tropical system is active in the Caribbean, Barranquilla typically sees multi-day heavy rain events and Port Puerto Colombia and Salgar beaches have dangerous surf. Check Caribbean weather forecasts if you are planning coastal activities during this window.

title: Month-by-month guide: December to March is dry, April to May is short rains, June to July Veranillo, August to November is peak rain
tidbit: Barranquilla's year in practice: December through March (dry, hot, Carnival in February, best visitor window). April to May (first short rain season, streets can flood). Late June to mid-July (Veranillo de San Juan, brief dry break, about 6 rain days in July). August through November (main rainy season, October is the worst month with about 280 mm and 15 rain days, flooding risk highest). No month is truly cold, temperatures stay 28 to 33 degrees year-round. The dry season is busier and more expensive for accommodation; book ahead from December to February.

title: Late June and early July bring a brief dry break called the Veranillo de San Juan: 2 to 4 weeks of sunshine
tidbit: Between the two rainy seasons, the coast experiences a mini-dry spell known as the Veranillo de San Juan, typically in the last week of June through mid-July. Rainfall drops sharply and temperatures feel more bearable without the oppressive humidity of the main wet season. The veranillo is not guaranteed every year and its length varies. When it arrives it is the most comfortable outdoor window between Carnival season and the next dry spell in December. Good time to visit coastal day trips and outdoor attractions.

title: 183 days makes you a tax resident
tidbit: Spend 183 days or more in Colombia in any rolling 365-day window and you become a tax resident the following year, owing DIAN a return on worldwide income. Budget COP 600,000 to 2,000,000 (USD 150 to 500) for a contador; the US has no tax treaty with Colombia (use the FEIE and Foreign Tax Credit).

title: Apostille and translate documents before you arrive
tidbit: Foreign documents (birth/marriage certificates, pension letters) need an apostille from your home country plus an official Spanish translation by a translator on the Cancilleria's list. Apostilles take weeks and a regular translation gets rejected. The most common cause of a visa rejection.

title: Which visa fits you, in one line each
tidbit: Government or military pension or social security: M Pensionado (3x minimum wage, about COP 5,252,715 in 2026). Passive or self-employed income: M Rentista (10x, about COP 17,509,050). Salaried remote employee of a foreign company: V Digital Nomad. Buying property: M Investor. Married to a Colombian: M Partner.

title: Close car and taxi doors gently, never slam them
tidbit: Colombians treat their cars with care. Closing a door the firm way many North Americans do reads as slamming, and taxi and rideshare drivers often react with real anger. Shut it softly.

title: Pharmacies deliver to your door, often free
tidbit: Almost every chain and corner drogueria delivers (domicilio), frequently free and within about 30 minutes, by phone or via the Rappi app (search farmacia). Always ask for the generic.
say_es: Lo tienen generico? Me lo pueden enviar a domicilio?
say_en: Do you have the generic? Can you deliver it to my home?

title: Eat the menu del dia at midday
tidbit: Ask for the menu del dia (or ejecutivo) at lunch and you get a full set meal (soup, a protein, rice, beans, salad, juice) for about COP 15,000 to 25,000 (USD 4 to 6). Eating your big meal at midday like locals do is the single biggest difference between a local food budget and a gringo one.
say_es: Cual es el menu del dia?
say_en: What is the set lunch today?

title: A taxi to urgencias often beats the public ambulance
tidbit: A public ambulance dispatched through 123 can take 20 to 45 minutes and will usually take you to the nearest public ER, not a private one. If the person can be moved safely, a taxi or DiDi to a private clinica's urgencias is frequently faster. Do not move anyone with a possible spinal injury, stroke, or heart attack.

title: EPS is 12.5% of declared income, not a flat fee
tidbit: Public health (EPS) costs 12.5% of your declared monthly income with a floor of one minimum wage, so a minimum declaration is about COP 220,000 per month (USD 55) in 2026. Pensioners pay 4% of their pension.
say_es: Quiero afiliarme como cotizante independiente. Cual es la IPS que me corresponde por mi direccion?
say_en: I want to enroll as a self-employed contributor. Which local clinic (IPS) am I assigned to by my address?

title: A private home-emergency membership is the real fast lane
tidbit: Memberships like EMI or Emermedica send a doctor-staffed vehicle to your home or the street, treat you on the spot, and arrange transfer, usually faster than the public line. They run roughly COP 40,000 to 90,000 per month (USD 10 to 22), cover the household, and have no age limit. Highest value for older adults and anyone living alone.

title: Ask about administracion and estrato before you sign
tidbit: Two costs change the rent math: administracion (a monthly building fee quoted separately, often COP 300,000 to 800,000 in a nicer tower) and estrato (1 to 6), which sets your utility tariffs, so a higher-estrato building means bigger electricity and gas bills. Both are on the paperwork.
say_es: La administracion esta incluida en el arriendo? Que estrato es? Piden codeudor o poliza?
say_en: Is the building fee included in the rent? What estrato is it? Do you require a co-signer or a lease-guarantee policy?

title: Always pay in COP, never the card-machine USD prompt
tidbit: When a card terminal or ATM asks if you want to pay in your home currency, always choose pesos. Dynamic currency conversion runs 6 to 10% worse than your bank's own rate.

title: Mark one account exempt from the 4x1000
tidbit: Colombia charges a 0.4% tax (the GMF, or cuatro por mil) on most withdrawals and transfers. You can designate one savings account as exempt up to about 350 UVT per month (roughly COP 17,400,000 in 2026). Ask for it when you open the account.
say_es: Quiero marcar esta cuenta de ahorros como exenta del 4x1000, por favor.
say_en: I want to mark this savings account as exempt from the 4x1000 tax, please.

title: Nequi works on a passport, before your cedula
tidbit: You cannot open a normal Colombian bank account as a tourist, but Nequi accepts passport-only signup, so you can have a working peso wallet and a virtual card within minutes of landing. Open a real bank account on top once your cedula arrives.

title: At a Servibanca ATM, always decline the offered exchange rate
tidbit: When you withdraw cash from a Servibanca ATM with a foreign card, the machine offers to convert at its own rate (dynamic currency conversion). Always decline it and choose to be charged in pesos. Accepting can add roughly USD 45 on an USD 800 withdrawal, about 5 to 6% on top.

title: Register a property wire as Form 4, not Form 5
tidbit: When you wire money in to buy property, your Colombian bank must process it as a Declaracion de Cambio Formulario 4 (foreign direct investment), not Form 5 (which is for living-expense transfers). Form 4 is what lets you legally repatriate the capital and gains when you sell. Confirm it with the bank before the wire arrives.
say_es: Voy a recibir una transferencia internacional para comprar un inmueble. Necesito que la registren como inversion extranjera directa con el Formulario 4.
say_en: I am receiving an international transfer to buy property. Register it as foreign direct investment using Form 4.

title: File a theft report (denuncia) online before you leave
tidbit: For travel insurance or to replace a stolen passport you need a denuncia. The fastest route is the national police site ADenunciar (adenunciar.policia.gov.co), Spanish-only, about 24 hours to process. In person, go to a Fiscalia URI, or call the Policia de Turismo for English help.
say_es: Quiero poner una denuncia por hurto. Me robaron el celular.
say_en: I want to file a report for theft. They stole my phone.

title: No dar papaya, the local safety mindset
tidbit: The one rule paisas live by: do not hand someone an easy opportunity. No phone on the cafe table, no flashing cash or jewelry, no walking drunk and alone late. Avoid being the easy target and you avoid most crime here.
say_es: No dar papaya.
say_en: Do not give papaya (do not make yourself an easy target).

title: Scopolamine and the dating-app robbery setup
tidbit: The risk that actually targets foreigners: a dating-app match steers you to a bar or your apartment, then drugs you with scopolamine (burundanga) to rob you. The US Embassy linked a cluster of foreigner deaths in Medellin to this in late 2023. Meet new dates in public early on, never bring a stranger home, and watch your drink poured.

title: Insist on the meter, or agree the fare first
tidbit: In a street taxi insist on the meter, or confirm the price before getting in; fixed quotes to foreigners often run 40 to 60% over. On the coast (Cartagena, Barranquilla) taxis are often unmetered, so agree the fare before the car moves.
say_es: Con taximetro, por favor. Voy a [direccion], barrio [nombre].
say_en: On the meter, please. I am going to [address], [neighborhood] neighborhood.
